abstract: Selective alkene epoxidation remains a critical challenge in sustainable chemical manufacturing due to the high energy requirements and low efficiency of conventional methods. In this study, we report a highly efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) system for the epoxidation of diverse alkenes using an $\alpha$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ (hematite) photoanode and a Br$^-$/BrO$^-$ redox mediator. By utilizing water as the sole oxygen source, this strategy achieves exceptional epoxide selectivity (up to >99%) and Faradaic efficiencies (up to 82 $\pm$ 4%), significantly outperforming existing direct electrochemical and PEC oxidation benchmarks. Mechanistic investigations—including isotope labeling, Hammett linear free-energy relationship analysis, and comparative studies with TiO$_2$ and Pt electrodes—reveal a unique non-radical pathway. On the $\alpha$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ surface, bromide is oxidized via oxygen atom transfer (OAT) from high-valent iron-oxo species to form hypobromite (BrO$^-$), which subsequently transfers the oxygen atom to the alkene substrate in the bulk solution. This mediated approach bypasses the radical-induced side reactions and thermodynamic limitations typical of direct oxidation. Our findings demonstrate a robust and energy-efficient PEC platform for the simultaneous production of value-added epoxides and hydrogen, offering a promising route for integrated solar-to-chemical energy conversion.
